section.title: The Fall of Babylon
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 18 completes the Babylon sequence begun in chapter 17, moving from interpretation to a full funeral dirge over the great city's sudden fall, sung in turn by an angel, a heavenly voice, the kings of the earth, the merchants, and the sailors of the world, before heaven itself is called to rejoice. It is bounded by the descending angel's cry that Babylon the great has fallen and the mighty angel's symbolic millstone at the chapter's close, and it stands in deliberate, stark contrast to the marriage of the Lamb announced immediately afterward in chapter 19.
section.thesis: Babylon the great, drunk on luxury and complicit in the blood of the saints, falls in a single hour under God's remembered judgment, mourned by the kings, merchants, and sailors who profited from her, even as heaven is summoned to rejoice, because her fall clears the way for the marriage of the Lamb.
section.frameNote: Six beats: an angel's cry that Babylon has fallen; a heavenly voice calling God's people to come out of her before her plagues arrive; the kings of the earth weeping at her smoke; the merchants mourning the loss of her luxury trade, cargo by cargo; the sailors lamenting from a distance; and heaven summoned to rejoice as a mighty angel casts her down like a great millstone, never to be found again.

units.label: The angel's cry: Babylon the great has fallen
units.note: a lair for demons and every unclean spirit

verses.text: After this I saw another angel descending from heaven with great authority, and the earth was illuminated by his glory.

And he cried out in a mighty voice: “{{epesen|Fallen, fallen}} is {{babylon|Babylon}} the great! She has become a lair for demons and a haunt for every unclean spirit, every unclean bird, and every detestable beast.

All the nations have drunk the wine of the passion of her immorality. The kings of the earth were immoral with her, and the {{emporos|merchants}} of the earth have grown wealthy from the extravagance of her {{strenos|luxury}}.”

units.label: Come out of her, My people
units.note: her sins piled up to heaven

verses.text: Then I heard another voice from heaven say: “{{exerchomai|Come out}} of her, My people, so that you will not share in her sins or contract any of her plagues.
For her sins are piled up to heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities.
Give back to her as she has done to others; pay her back double for what she has done; mix her a double portion in her own cup.
As much as she has glorified herself and lived in luxury, give her the same measure of torment and grief. In her heart she says, ‘I sit as queen; I am not a widow and will never see grief.’
Therefore her plagues will come in one day, death and grief and famine, and she will be consumed by fire, for mighty is the Lord God who judges her.”

units.label: The kings of the earth weep
units.note: in a single hour your judgment has come

verses.text: Then the kings of the earth who committed sexual immorality and lived in luxury with her will weep and wail at the sight of the smoke rising from the fire that consumes her.
In fear of her torment, they will stand at a distance and cry out: “Woe, woe to the great city, the mighty city of Babylon! For in a single hour your judgment has come.”

units.label: The merchants mourn her cargo
units.note: no one left to buy their goods

verses.text: And the merchants of the earth will weep and mourn over her, because there is no one left to buy their cargo:
cargo of gold, silver, precious stones, and pearls; of fine linen, purple, silk, and scarlet; of all kinds of citron wood and every article of ivory, precious wood, bronze, iron, and marble;
of cinnamon, spice, incense, myrrh, and frankincense; of wine, olive oil, fine flour, and wheat; of cattle, sheep, horses, and chariots; of slaves and souls of men.
And they will say: “The fruit of your soul's desire has departed from you; all your luxury and splendor have vanished, never to be seen again.”
The merchants who sold these things and grew their wealth from her will stand at a distance, in fear of her torment. They will weep and mourn,
saying: “Woe, woe to the great city, clothed in fine linen and purple and scarlet, adorned with gold and precious stones and pearls!

units.label: The sailors lament from a distance
units.note: what city was ever like this

verses.text: For in a single hour such fabulous wealth has been destroyed!” Every shipmaster, passenger, and sailor, and all who make their living from the sea, will stand at a distance
and cry out at the sight of the smoke rising from the fire that consumes her. “What city was ever like this great city?” they will exclaim.

Then they will throw dust on their heads as they weep and mourn and cry out: “Woe, woe to the great city, where all who had ships on the sea were enriched by her wealth! For in a single hour she has been destroyed.”

units.label: Heaven rejoices, and the millstone is cast down

verses.text: Rejoice over her, O heaven, O saints and apostles and prophets, because God has pronounced for you His judgment against her.
Then a mighty angel picked up a stone the size of a great millstone and cast it into the sea, saying: “With such violence the great city of Babylon will be cast down, never to be seen again.

And the sound of harpists and musicians, of flute players and trumpeters, will never ring out in you again. Nor will any craftsmen of any trade be found in you again, nor the sound of a millstone be heard in you again.

The light of a lamp will never shine in you again, and the voices of a bride and bridegroom will never call out in you again. For your merchants were the great ones of the earth, because all the nations were deceived by your sorcery.”
And there was found in her the blood of prophets and saints, and of all who had been slain on the earth.

epesen.gloss: Fell, has fallen, the aorist of pipto. John doubles the verb, epesen, epesen, for emphatic, dirge-like effect, directly echoing the taunt song over historical Babylon in Isaiah 21:9. The doubled cry announces Babylon's fall as already, prophetically, an accomplished fact, even before the narrative that follows unpacks it.

babylon.gloss: The Greek form of the ancient city's name. In Revelation it names not the literal Mesopotamian city, long fallen by John's day, but a symbolic Babylon, an idolatrous, luxury-drunk, blood-guilty world power, standing in Scripture's long tradition of Babylon as the archetype of humanity organized in defiance of God, from Genesis 11 onward.

emporos.gloss: A merchant or trader. The word and its cognates dominate the chapter's central section, cataloguing Babylon's vast trade in luxury goods and, devastatingly, slaves and souls of men. Their grief is not for Babylon's sin but for their own lost profit, exposing an economy built on exploitation as complicit in her judgment.

strenos.gloss: Luxury, sensuous excess, wanton self-indulgence. Related to the verb streniao, to live in reckless extravagance, the word appears repeatedly across the chapter to describe Babylon's manner of life; her judgment is measured out in exact proportion to it, the same measure of torment and grief she gave herself in luxury.

exerchomai.gloss: To come out, to go out. The heavenly voice's command, come out of her, My people, draws on the same verb used of Israel's exodus from Egypt and the later Old Testament call to flee Babylon, Jeremiah 51:45, Isaiah 48:20, casting Babylon's judgment as a fresh occasion for God's people to separate themselves from complicity in her sins.

basic.context: An angel announces that Babylon the great has fallen, and a voice from heaven calls God's people to come out of her before her sudden judgment arrives. What follows is a threefold dirge: the kings, the merchants, and the sailors of the world all watch her smoke from a distance and mourn, each grieving the loss of what she gave them, wealth, luxury, and profit, rather than grieving her sin. The chapter ends with heaven summoned to rejoice, and a mighty angel casts a great millstone into the sea to picture Babylon's sudden, final, irreversible fall.
basic.hermeneutics: Christians disagree on whether this fall points mainly to a past historical judgment, most obviously the collapse of pagan Rome, or to a still-future or ongoing pattern of judgment on every Babylon-like system of idolatrous wealth and power throughout history. The debate matters less than the chapter's clear moral center: whatever wears Babylon's character, an economy and culture built on exploitation, idolatry, and violence against God's people, will be judged suddenly and completely, and God's people are called to come out of it now.
basic.originalLanguages: The angel's cry doubles the Greek verb epesen, fallen, fallen, turning Babylon's judgment into an Old Testament-style taunt song. Her name, Babylon, gathers up Scripture's whole story of organized human defiance of God since Genesis 11. Her strenos, her reckless luxury, becomes the very measure of her judgment, and the emporoi, the merchants, mourn their lost trade more than her sin. And the call to exerchomai, come out, echoes Israel's ancient exodus from Egypt and the prophets' call to flee historical Babylon.
basic.typology: Babylon's sudden, complete fall, pictured as a great millstone cast into the sea, never to be found again, fulfills and intensifies Jeremiah's own symbolic act of sinking a scroll of judgment against historical Babylon in the Euphrates, showing the certainty and finality of divine judgment on organized evil.
basic.christ: The chapter's dirges over lost wealth stand in sharp, deliberate contrast to the wedding announced in the next chapter; Babylon's fall clears the world's stage for the marriage of the Lamb, showing that Christ's joy, not the world's luxury, is the true and lasting treasure.
basic.god: God is shown as the Judge who remembers sins piled up to heaven and repays exactly, in the same measure given, yet He is also the God who warns His people beforehand, come out of her, My people, so that judgment on Babylon is never meant to catch His own off guard.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 21 and Jeremiah 50-51 supply the taunt song and the command to flee historical Babylon that this chapter echoes. Ezekiel 27 supplies the cargo-list lament over Tyre that shapes the merchants' dirge here. And Revelation 19 supplies the wedding of the Lamb that immediately answers Babylon's fall with joy.
basic.oneStory: From Babel's tower to Babylon's exile to Rome's empire, Scripture tells one story of human civilization organizing itself in luxury, idolatry, and violence against God's people, and always, eventually, falling. This chapter's dirge is Scripture's last word on that pattern: come out of her now, because her fall is certain, sudden, and final, and it clears the way for the marriage of the Lamb.

context.literary: Chapter 18 is structured as an extended dirge, or funeral lament, a genre borrowed directly from Old Testament oracles against nations, especially Ezekiel 26-27's lament over Tyre and Isaiah 13-14, 21's taunts against Babylon, voiced in turn by an angel, a heavenly voice, the kings, the merchants, and the sailors, before a final call for heaven to rejoice and a symbolic enacted judgment closes the chapter.
context.historical: For John's first readers under Rome, the chapter's picture of a city whose merchants grew rich trading in luxury goods, including human slaves and souls, so accurately described first-century Rome's economy that many hear a direct, courageous critique of the empire's wealth and its cost in human suffering, warning believers not to profit from or be seduced by that system even while it appeared unshakeable.
context.canonical: The chapter gathers the Old Testament's oracles against Babylon, Tyre, and Nineveh into one climactic lament, while its call to come out of her, My people echoes the exodus and the return from exile, and its position immediately before the marriage of the Lamb in chapter 19 makes it the last dark scene before Revelation turns fully toward joy and consummation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce, with the certainty of prophecy already fulfilled, that Babylon's fall is sure and sudden, to expose the moral bankruptcy of an economy and culture built on luxury and exploitation, and to call God's people to separate themselves from complicity in her sins before her judgment falls.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Babylon's certain fall and the world's mourning over lost wealth; it does not prescribe a specific modern nation or company to identify as Babylon. Its abiding truths are that idolatrous, exploitative systems will be judged suddenly and completely, and that God's people are called, now, to come out from under such systems' sway rather than profit from or depend on them.

debates.question: Is Babylon's fall a past, historical judgment, or a future or ongoing pattern?

views.label: A past judgment, fulfilled in the fall of Rome
views.case: Since Babylon's description matches first-century Rome so closely, seven hills, vast trade, persecution of the saints, many read this chapter's fulfillment in the historical collapse of pagan Rome, traditionally associated with the sack of Rome in AD 410, a real, dateable judgment within history that vindicated the martyrs.

views.label: A future or ongoing pattern, not yet finally fulfilled
views.case: Others note that Rome's actual decline was gradual, not the sudden, single-hour catastrophe described here, and that no historical fall has matched every detail of the chapter, so they read Babylon's judgment as either still future, at history's end, or as a repeating pattern fulfilled partially in every subsequent Babylon-like power, awaiting one final, complete fulfillment.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the chapter is not empty poetry: some real Babylon, first-century Rome at minimum, has already tasted this judgment in part, and Scripture's pattern gives every reader confidence that any future or present Babylon will meet the same certain end.

debates.question: Should believers hear come out of her, My people as a call to physical separation, or something else?

views.label: A call to moral and spiritual separation, not necessarily physical relocation
views.case: Most interpreters read this primarily as a summons to refuse complicity in Babylon's sins and idolatries, wherever a believer physically lives, rather than a literal command to relocate cities, paralleling Paul's call to be in the world but not of it.

views.label: A call that may include literal withdrawal from corrupt systems
views.case: Others stress that the command is concrete and active, urging believers to genuinely disentangle their economic and social lives from exploitative or idolatrous systems where possible, not merely to hold a private inward attitude of separation.

debates.bearing: Either way, the command is not passive; it calls for active, costly distinction from Babylon's values and practices, whatever a believer's literal address.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with a doubled cry, epesen, epesen, fallen, fallen, the same emphatic dirge form used by Isaiah against historical Babylon, now declaring the fall of Babylon the great as an accomplished, certain fact. Her very name, Babylon, gathers the whole biblical story of organized human rebellion against God traced back to Babel in Genesis 11. Her characteristic sin is strenos, reckless luxury and self-indulgence, and God's judgment is measured out in the identical proportion she measured for herself, torment for luxury, grief for glory. The emporoi, the merchants of the earth, catalogue in relentless detail the cargo they will no longer sell, culminating devastatingly in slaves and souls of men, showing an economy whose wealth was inseparable from human exploitation. And running through the whole chapter is the divine summons to exerchomai, come out, the same verb behind Israel's exodus and the prophets' call to flee historical Babylon, now given final, urgent force before her sudden fall.

typology.type: The taunt song over historical Babylon and Tyre, Isaiah 13-14, 21, Ezekiel 26-27
typology.fulfillment: The dirge of Revelation 18, gathering every Old Testament lament over a fallen, idolatrous city into their final, complete form.

typology.type: Jeremiah's scroll of judgment sunk in the Euphrates, Jeremiah 51:59-64
typology.fulfillment: The mighty angel's millstone cast into the sea, Revelation 18:21, a deliberate echo enacting the same certain, irreversible judgment on the final Babylon.

typology.type: God's call for His people to flee Babylon, Jeremiah 51:45, Isaiah 48:20
typology.fulfillment: The heavenly voice's command, come out of her, My people, Revelation 18:4, urging final, decisive separation before judgment falls.

study.christ: This chapter never mentions Jesus by name, yet it exists entirely to clear the stage for Him: Babylon's dirge is Scripture's last dark note before Revelation turns, in the very next chapter, to the marriage supper of the Lamb. Babylon offers luxury, splendor, and profit that vanish in a single hour; Christ offers a wedding and a kingdom that cannot be shaken. The contrast is the whole point, everything Babylon promised and could not keep, the Lamb actually gives, and keeps forever.
study.god: God is exact in judgment, repaying Babylon double for what she has done and giving her the same measure of torment she gave herself in glory, yet He is equally exact in mercy, warning His people to come out before that judgment falls rather than leaving them to guess. His remembering of her sins, piled up to heaven, shows that no exploitation or idolatry, however normalized or profitable, escapes His notice.

crossReferences.note: Fallen, fallen is Babylon is quoted almost verbatim from this earlier oracle against historical Babylon.
Come out of her, my people echoes this earlier call for Israel to flee judgment on Babylon, applied here to a final, greater Babylon.
The detailed cargo list lamented by Tyre's trading partners is the model for the merchants' catalogue of lost trade in this chapter.
The marriage supper of the Lamb, announced immediately after Babylon's fall, the deliberate positive counterpart to this chapter's dirge.

study.oneStory: Every empire that dresses rebellion against God in luxury and calls it glory eventually hears this dirge sung over it. Revelation 18 is Scripture's last and fullest telling of a story begun at Babel: human power organizes itself against God, grows rich on exploitation, and falls suddenly when God remembers its sins. But the call embedded even in judgment, come out of her, My people, is itself an act of grace, and the chapter's darkness exists only to clear the way for the wedding of the Lamb that follows.

sermon.title: Revelation (Part 20): Revelation 17-18
sermon.why: Carson's sermon directly covers Revelation 18, describing it as a dirge or lament over Babylon's fall in which she receives exactly what she deserves, matching this study's passage and theme precisely.

quiz.q: Why do the kings, merchants, and sailors mourn Babylon's fall?

options.text: They grieve the loss of the wealth, luxury, and profit she gave them, not her sin against God.
options.note: Revelation 18:9-19 shows their grief is entirely for lost wealth and trade.

options.text: They grieve because they loved her righteousness and feared losing a godly example.
options.note: Babylon is condemned as sinful and blood-guilty, not righteous.

options.text: They mourn because they expect to be judged alongside her.
options.note: Their laments focus on lost profit and luxury, not fear of their own judgment.

options.text: They do not mourn at all; the chapter describes only celebration.
options.note: The chapter describes extensive weeping and wailing from the kings, merchants, and sailors.

quiz.q: What does the heavenly voice command God's people to do in verse 4?

options.text: Come out of her, so that they do not share in her sins or receive her plagues.
options.note: Revelation 18:4 gives this command directly.

options.text: Stay in Babylon to witness to her merchants.
options.note: The command is to come out, not remain.

options.text: Join her feast to demonstrate Christian freedom.
options.note: The command is separation from her sins, not participation.

options.text: Wait for the angel's permission before making any response.
options.note: The command is immediate and direct, not conditional on further permission.

quiz.q: How is Babylon's judgment measured out, according to verse 7?

options.text: In the same measure she glorified herself and lived in luxury, that same measure of torment and grief is given back to her.
options.note: Revelation 18:7 states this exact principle of proportional judgment.

options.text: Far less severely than her actual sins deserved.
options.note: The text describes exact, proportional repayment, not leniency.

options.text: Randomly, without any relation to what she had done.
options.note: The judgment is explicitly measured against her own self-glorification and luxury.

options.text: She receives no judgment at all in this chapter.
options.note: The whole chapter describes her sudden and severe judgment.

quiz.q: What does the mighty angel's millstone, thrown into the sea, picture?

options.text: The sudden, violent, and irreversible fall of Babylon, never to be found again.
options.note: Revelation 18:21 gives this exact interpretation of the enacted sign.

options.text: A blessing given to Babylon's faithful remnant.
options.note: The millstone pictures destruction, not blessing.

options.text: The rebuilding of Babylon after a period of judgment.
options.note: The text insists she will never be found again, ruling out rebuilding.

options.text: A literal execution method used against her merchants.
options.note: The millstone is a symbolic sign-act picturing the city's fall, not a literal execution.

quiz.q: What is found in Babylon at the chapter's close, verse 24?

options.text: The blood of prophets and saints, and of all who had been slain on the earth.
options.note: Revelation 18:24 states this as the final indictment against her.

options.text: A remnant of righteous worshipers spared from judgment.
options.note: The verse describes the guilt found in her, not a spared remnant.

options.text: Treasures later restored to the nations.
options.note: The chapter describes total loss of her wealth, not restoration.

options.text: Nothing; the city is described as entirely empty of any remains.
options.note: The text specifically names what was found in her, the blood of the slain.

questions: The kings, merchants, and sailors weep for what Babylon gave them, not for her sin. Where might you find yourself more attached to what a corrupt system provides you than concerned about the harm it causes others?

Come out of her, My people is a call to active separation, not passive distance. What would it look like this week to actually step back from something that quietly makes you complicit in exploitation or idolatry?
Babylon's merchants trade in everything, down to slaves and souls of men. Where do you see people treated as commodities today, and how might you respond differently than the world's economy does?

Heaven is summoned to rejoice at Babylon's fall, because God has pronounced judgment for His saints, not merely against Babylon. How does it change your view of God's judgment to know it vindicates His suffering people?

This whole dark chapter exists to clear the stage for the wedding of the Lamb in chapter 19. When you feel weighed down by the world's corruption, how does looking ahead to that wedding feast give you hope?

translationNotes.note: Fallen, fallen translates a doubled Greek verb, epesen epesen, a deliberate Hebraic dirge form quoting Isaiah 21:9 almost word for word; some translations render it as a single has fallen, but the doubling intensifies the finality of the announcement.
Luxury and related phrases translate strenos and its cognate verb, sometimes rendered sensuality or wantonness in other versions, capturing not mere comfort but reckless, self-indulgent excess.

The cargo list is deliberately exhaustive, moving from precious metals and stones through fabrics, foods, and livestock to, devastatingly, slaves and souls of men at the very end, a translation choice, some versions render simply bodies, that makes plain the list's final horror, human trafficking.

Come out of her uses the same Greek verb, exerchomai, found in the Greek Old Testament's call for Israel to leave Egypt and Babylon; translators consistently preserve this exodus echo across versions.

meta.notes: BSB text supplied pre-verified this session. Several original em dashes in this chapter were converted to commas or colons before being given, per the task instructions; no further em or en dashes were introduced. The angel's cry in verses 2-3 is rendered as one continuous quotation, since the source text's quotation marks span both verses as a single utterance. Poetic line breaks, where present in the source, were flattened into flowing prose per this repo's convention.
